questions.question_text: A homebuyer discovers that a broker intentionally concealed a known foundation defect. Under Chapter 93A, the buyer may seek up to:
questions.explanation: For willful or knowing violations — or a bad-faith refusal to settle after the demand letter — Chapter 93A requires multiple damages of at least two and up to three times actual damages (MGL c.93A §9(3)). Reasonable attorney fees are also awarded to prevailing plaintiffs.

questions.question_text: Before filing a Chapter 93A lawsuit, a consumer must first:
distractors: File a complaint with the Board
questions.explanation: Chapter 93A requires a 30-day demand letter before filing suit, giving the defendant a chance to settle. Failure to send the demand letter can result in dismissal of the case.

questions.question_text: Chapter 93A prohibits which type of conduct in trade or commerce?
questions.correct_answer: Unfair or deceptive acts or practices
questions.explanation: Chapter 93A broadly prohibits 'unfair or deceptive acts or practices' in trade or commerce. This is broader than fraud — it covers negligent misrepresentation and deceptive omissions too.

questions.question_text: Which government official can independently enforce Chapter 93A violations?
questions.explanation: The Attorney General can enforce Chapter 93A independently, in addition to consumers filing private actions. The AG's Consumer Protection Division handles systemic violations.

questions.question_text: A prevailing plaintiff in a Chapter 93A real estate case may be awarded which additional relief beyond damages?
distractors: Free title insurance on the property
questions.explanation: Chapter 93A requires courts to award reasonable attorney fees and costs to prevailing plaintiffs, irrespective of the amount in controversy (§9(4)). This mandatory fee-shifting encourages consumers to enforce their rights even for moderate-value claims.

questions.question_text: Unlike most states, Massachusetts does NOT require sellers to provide which document?
distractors: A lead paint disclosure for pre-1978 homes
questions.explanation: Massachusetts has no mandatory standardized seller disclosure form. However, brokers — and sellers acting in trade or commerce, such as developers — can be liable under Chapter 93A for failing to disclose known material defects (940 CMR 3.16(2)). A private owner selling their own home is generally outside 93A (Lantner v. Carson) but may still face common-law misrepresentation liability.

questions.question_text: A seller knows the basement floods every spring but tells the buyer the basement is dry. This is an example of:
questions.explanation: Knowingly stating a false material fact is intentional misrepresentation (deceit), exposing the seller to common-law fraud liability and possible rescission. When the false statement comes from a party in trade or commerce — a broker, developer, or business seller — Chapter 93A liability (double-to-treble damages) also applies, and a licensee would additionally face Board discipline.

questions.question_text: A licensee negligently tells a buyer that a property has city sewer when it actually has a septic system. The licensee may face:
distractors: No liability since it was unintentional
Only Board discipline, not civil damages
Only civil liability, not Board action
questions.explanation: Misrepresentation — whether intentional or negligent — can result in both civil liability under Chapter 93A and Board discipline. Negligence does not excuse a licensee from responsibility.

questions.question_text: Which of the following would be considered a material fact requiring disclosure in Massachusetts?
questions.explanation: Material facts include structural defects, environmental hazards, zoning violations, and title issues. A seller's motivation and neighbor details are not material facts. Tax value is public information.

questions.question_text: Although Massachusetts has no mandatory seller disclosure form, liability for non-disclosure arises primarily under:
questions.explanation: Without a mandatory disclosure form, Chapter 93A — via the AG's regulation 940 CMR 3.16(2) — is the primary vehicle for holding brokers and business sellers liable for failing to disclose known material defects. (Private one-off home sellers fall outside 93A but face common-law misrepresentation claims.)

questions.question_text: A licensee's laptop containing client financial data is stolen. Under Massachusetts law, the licensee must:
questions.correct_answer: Protect and report the data breach
distractors: Take no action unless clients complain
Only notify the Board of Registration
questions.explanation: Licensees must protect confidential personal information. Massachusetts data breach notification law requires prompt notification of affected individuals when personal information is compromised.

questions.question_text: The Board of Registration can impose discipline for Chapter 93A violations:
distractors: Only after a court finds 93A liability
Only if the AG files charges first
questions.explanation: The Board disciplines licensees under license law (MGL c.112 §87AAA) for the same misconduct that violates Chapter 93A — misrepresentation, dishonest dealing — independently of any civil suit. Board discipline and 93A civil liability are separate tracks that can proceed in parallel.

questions.question_text: Which entity handles systemic consumer protection violations in the real estate industry?
questions.explanation: The Attorney General's Consumer Protection Division handles systemic violations of Chapter 93A. The Board handles individual licensee discipline, while the AG addresses broader industry patterns.

questions.question_text: A licensee stores client Social Security numbers in an unencrypted file on a shared computer. This practice violates:
distractors: No law, it is a best practice only
questions.explanation: Licensees must protect confidential personal information in written, verbal, or electronic form. Storing sensitive data unencrypted on a shared computer violates Massachusetts data protection requirements.

questions.question_text: A consumer believes a broker engaged in deceptive practices but the dollar amount is small. Chapter 93A still provides an incentive to sue because:
questions.correct_answer: Attorney fees are awarded to prevailing plaintiffs
distractors: The Board pays the consumer's legal costs
Small claims court has no filing fees
questions.explanation: Chapter 93A's fee-shifting provision (attorney fees awarded to prevailing plaintiffs) plus treble damages make it economically viable to bring even moderate-value consumer protection claims.
